steps.title: From Mission to Picture
steps.body:
Yesterday you started exploring your marriage mission — the core purpose of what you're building together.

Today, we turn that mission into a vision. A picture of what your marriage looks like when it's working at its best.

A vision is specific. It's not 'we want to be happy.'

It's: 'We want a home where we laugh every day, where we handle conflict within hours not days, where we prioritize our relationship even when the kids are young, and where we dream together about our future.'

Vision answers the question: if our marriage were thriving in every dimension, what would we actually see, hear, and feel?

Most couples drift because they never decide where they're going.

A vision is deciding.

It's not predicting the future.
It's designing it — together.

questions.label:
Close your eyes for a moment. Fast-forward one year.

Your marriage is genuinely thriving — not perfect, but alive, connected, and working.

What do you see?

• How do you greet each other at the end of the day?
• What do your conversations sound like?
• How do you handle a disagreement?
• What do you do together for fun?
• How do you make decisions about money and time?

Write the picture. Get specific.

steps.title: The Vision Conversation Framework
steps.body:
A shared vision isn't something you create alone. It's something you build together.

Here's a simple framework for the conversation:

1. Each of you writes your vision separately — what a thriving marriage looks like to you

2. Share and listen — no editing, no correcting. Just 'tell me more about that.'

3. Find overlap — what do both of you want? Where do your visions naturally align?

4. Honor differences — where you diverge, ask: 'How could we do BOTH?' or 'What matters most to you about that?'

5. Create one combined vision — not perfectly aligned, but genuinely shared.

The goal isn't total agreement. The goal is that both of you see yourselves in the picture.

steps.title: The Gap Between Current and Ideal

questions.label:
Think about the vision you just wrote. Now look at your current reality.

Where's the biggest gap?

• Communication?
• Quality time?
• Physical intimacy?
• Shared purpose?
• How you handle conflict?

Name the gap. Then ask: 'What's one thing we could do this week that would close that gap by even 10%?'

steps.question: When sharing your vision with your partner, the best approach is:
options: Explain why your vision is better than theirs
Share your vision, then ask what resonates for them
Wait for the perfect vision before sharing anything
Let them share first so you can adjust yours
feedback: A vision conversation isn't a debate. The goal isn't right vs wrong.
Yes. Share openly, then invite their perspective. The combined vision comes from mutual exploration, not negotiation.
Perfectionism kills vision. Share what you have, even if it's rough.
Share authentically, not strategically. Your genuine vision matters.

steps.title: Connecting the Vision to the Present
steps.body:
Vision: 'We want to feel like a team who has each other's backs.'

That's beautiful, but it floats in the air until you connect it to something concrete.

Connected to daily life: 'Having each other's backs means: when one of us is stressed, the other says 'What do you need?' instead of 'You're not the only one stressed.' It means before a hard conversation, we take a breath and do a soft startup. It means we check in every day for five minutes.'

Your vision is your destination. Your daily behaviors are the path. Make the path visible.

steps.title: Share Your Vision
steps.prompt:
Tonight, share the vision you wrote with your partner. Here's how to start:

'I spent some time thinking about what I want our marriage to look like. I'd love to share it with you and hear yours too.'

After they share, your job is ONLY to listen. No correcting. No explaining why your version is better. Just: 'Thank you for sharing that. Tell me more about...'

Write what you're most curious to hear from their vision:
steps.body:
Tonight, share the vision you wrote with your partner. Here's how to start:

'I spent some time thinking about what I want our marriage to look like. I'd love to share it with you and hear yours too.'

After they share, your job is ONLY to listen. No correcting. No explaining why your version is better. Just: 'Thank you for sharing that. Tell me more about...'

Write what you're most curious to hear from their vision:

steps.question: A shared vision must be perfectly aligned between both partners to be useful.
feedback: Correct. Perfect alignment isn't realistic. What matters is that both partners see themselves in the vision and can point in the same general direction. Differences create richness, not conflict — if you talk about them.
Not quite. Useful visions have room for both perspectives. Perfect alignment is a fantasy. What's real and valuable is understanding each other's vision and finding where they overlap.

steps.title: Tomorrow: Designing Your Marriage Practices
steps.body:
Vision is your destination. But a destination without a vehicle is just a dream.

Tomorrow: the specific practices, habits, and commitments that will carry your vision into real life.

steps.title: Day Complete
steps.body: Great work today! You're building something real.
